headline: MILK DROPS 5.4%, CATTLE COMPLEX FOLLOWS LOWER
subheadline: Class III milk breaks through $16 on processing concerns while screwworm fears hit cattle futures.
lead: Class III milk fell 5.4% to $16.14, the biggest single-session drop in three weeks, as processing plant disruptions spread beyond the ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began Tuesday May 19. Live cattle dropped 2% to $236.72 and lean hogs fell 2.7% to $96.15, confirming yesterday's call that processing problems would hit milk first, cattle complex next. The screwworm outbreak now confirmed in New Mexico adds supply uncertainty to an already constrained processing chain.
the_takeaway: Processing bottlenecks are hitting livestock prices harder than supply fundamentals justify.
teaser: Milk falls sharply on processing fears while screwworm spreads beyond Texas borders.

one_number.unit: additional screwworm cases confirmed by USDA
one_number.context: USDA confirmed three more New World screwworm cases Monday, including the first outside Texas in New Mexico. The outbreak is driving cattle market volatility as supply concerns mount, even though the disease primarily affects wound healing rather than herd mortality. Market's pricing worst-case scenarios until containment strategy proves effective.

yesterdays_call.summary: Processing problems hit milk first, cattle complex next if it spreads.
yesterdays_call.note: Milk led lower exactly as called, with cattle and hogs following the processing disruption theme.

sections.title: MILK BREAKS $16 SUPPORT
sections.body: Class III milk **fell sharply to $16.14**, losing 5.4% in the biggest single-session drop since mid-May. The break through $16 support confirms processing plant disruptions are spreading beyond Cargill's locked-out facilities, with **cheese block prices weakening** as milk powder inventories back up at constrained plants. Milk's fall below $16 opens the door to $15.50, the February low that marked the last time processing capacity couldn't match milk flow. The complex is pricing supply chain problems, not milk supply itself.
sections.bottom_line: Milk's $16 break signals processing capacity can't handle current milk flow.
sections.farmer_action: Milk producers: lock pricing on Q3 production if $16 holds as resistance.

sections.title: CATTLE FALLS ON SCREWWORM SPREAD
sections.body: Live cattle dropped 2% to $236.72 as **USDA confirmed three additional screwworm cases**, including the first outside Texas in New Mexico. The outbreak is creating supply uncertainty even though screwworm affects wound healing, not mortality rates directly. Feeder cattle fell 0.9% to $350.70 as the **1.48 feeder-to-live ratio** holds near the tight end of its range, suggesting feedlot operators remain patient despite the disease concerns. The market's pricing containment failure until USDA's sterile fly strategy proves effective.
sections.catalyst: USDA confirmed first screwworm case outside Texas, in New Mexico
sections.bottom_line: Screwworm spread adds supply uncertainty to already strained processing capacity.

sections.title: GRAINS HOLD MODEST GAINS
sections.body: Corn ticked up 0.3% to $4.21, beans added 0.4% to $11.16, and wheat firmed 0.5% to $5.86 in **quiet trade** that broke the five-day slide without conviction. The complex is getting mild support from **Argentina's export tax cuts** on wheat and barley, dropping levies from 7.5% to 5.5% effective this month. December corn at $4.48 shows a **27-cent carry** that's working as old-crop supplies thin ahead of new-crop harvest pressure. Fund positioning remains light, no major directional commitment yet.
sections.catalyst: Argentina cuts export taxes on wheat and barley to 5.5%
sections.bottom_line: Grains pause their slide but lack conviction to build on modest gains.

outside_the_pit.title: Senate farm bill draft coming in June, markup in July
outside_the_pit.body: Senate Ag Chairman John Boozman confirms the Senate farm bill draft drops in June with markup scheduled for July. The timeline puts farm bill passage on track for late summer, assuming House and Senate can reconcile their approaches to crop insurance and conservation spending.

outside_the_pit.title: China's oil imports hit eight-year low in May
outside_the_pit.body: Chinese crude imports fell to 7.8 million barrels daily in May, the lowest since October 2017, as Iran-Hormuz tensions drove prices higher. The decline removes a key demand pillar for energy markets and could pressure crude further if the diplomatic progress continues.

outside_the_pit.title: Farmland values rise but working capital access tightens
outside_the_pit.body: American Farm Bureau analysis shows farmland values continue climbing while farmers struggle to access operating loans. The disconnect between asset values and cash flow availability is creating liquidity problems for producers despite strong balance sheet equity.

spread_to_watch.label: Class III milk / cheese block ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: Milk processing bottlenecks are backing up liquid milk faster than they're constraining cheese production, compressing the normal ratio. When processing capacity normalizes, this ratio should widen back toward 1.08, suggesting cheese block prices have more room to fall than milk futures.

basis.headline: Eastern corn basis firming on ethanol plant restarts
basis.body: **Eastern Corn Belt basis** is tightening as ethanol plants come back online after maintenance shutdowns. Producers from Ohio through Indiana are seeing **firm bids** relative to the July contract. Western Belt staying soft as river logistics remain strong and export pace stays moderate.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain complex break out of its five-day slide this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: Grains posted modest gains today but lack conviction. The slide paused on Argentina export tax news, but fund positioning remains light with no major directional commitment yet.

the_more_you_know.title: Processing capacity bottlenecks hit livestock differently
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 5.4% milk drop and 2% cattle decline show how processing constraints affect different livestock markets. Milk can't be stored long-term, so plant shutdowns immediately back up supply and fall sharply prices. Cattle can be held on feed longer, so processing disruptions show up as basis weakness before futures break. Hogs fall between the two: packing plant problems hit cash markets first, then drag futures lower as the backlog builds.

watch_list.desc: Weekly export sales: beef under 15K MT signals domestic processing problems spreading to export channels
USDA screwworm containment updates: failure to contain outbreak in New Mexico escalates cattle market risk premium
Milk futures at $15.50: break of February low opens path to $15.00 psychological support

daily_quote.text: The four most dangerous words in investing are: this time it's different.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA reports, industry news
